item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Solid concept, good execution, but the numbers are too off, that break it to me.

A velocity of 10 m/s is just 36 Km/h (less than 22.5 miles/hour), terminal velocity for a pine cone isn't much more than throwing them by hand, and the force of the explosion wasn't given. So, as far as I'm aware, this isn't nearly as dangerous as it is purported to be.

It isn't Keter, either. Even if you make it more dangerous than a modern ICBM its containment is quite reliable, and I see no reason to be otherwise. This is a solid Euclid.

You don't need to make this more dangerous. You can correct the numbers to really make it into a war-worthy missile, or you can go with the numbers and make it a freak flying tree with annoying dropping cones and a somewhat dangerous blast at the end. It works either way, if not better with the current low numbers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I certainly wasn't expecting my first scip to do this well, especially considering the idea behind it.
I have a tamarack in my backyard, and one day, while I was half-asleep in a hammock, I looked over at it and thought "You look pretty aerodynamic."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is… Interesting…
Them doing this as a means of reproduction is a nice touch though, and it Makes sense (Well, as much sense as missile launching trees are gonna). Definitely an upvote. im kind of curious to know what gave you the idea for this? Some SCPs are pretty obvious in thier inspiration, but this one is… well… I just have no idea what would give the idea of a ballistic tree.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like 10 m/s isn't as fast as you might think it is. That's only as fast as something which has fallen for about a second. For something to do significant damage to anything bigger or further away than a pine cone, you might want it to accelerate at 10 m/s2 for a few seconds up to something like 100 m/s for it to do the kind of damage described. You also mentioned that the cones are heat resistant, which implies they need to deal with heat, in which case you may want an even higher top speed so you start having to deal with the shockwave heating icbm type weapons have to deal with. You want these things going at least 50 Km into the air for them to need to deal with the kind of effects you've described them being designed to deal with.

item.title: Changes
item.content:encoded:
I have re-spaced the paragraphs, hopefully I've fixed the spacing issue somewhat.
I also changed the descriptions for Phases 3 and 8, since I don't think that one-or-two-word descriptions may end up leaving a bit of important information out.
Also fixed "stage" being used in the first paragraph rather than "Phase", so that one was a tad embarrassing.

Tell me if I made it worse, spacing isn't exactly my forte, but I'm practicing!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
conceptually, this is pretty great, and the tone is pretty solid, and overall the writing is pretty solid too. the spacing is quite… how do i put this… uneven? the paragraphs have weird lengths relative to each other, basically, though that isn't necessarily a deal-breaker. it could do with a bit more 'meat' to it - more content, in the sense that the idea could be explored more in-depth.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 for panicky tree missiles :D

You've got a bunch of paragraph spacing problems, tho. :B

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know it's independently derived, but partly reminds me of the Stage Trees in Larry Niven's Known Space mythology. Which actually launch themselves into space to spread their seeds to other planets and star systems.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Tamarack tree -> tomahawk missile?

Yah, I know it's a cruise missile, not ballistic, just wondering why that tree :p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Trees actually can talk to each other!:D
http://e360.yale.edu/features/exploring_how_and_why_trees_talk_to_each_other
Not really unknown, though given it's a missile tree, this strain might have a slightly different system.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well isn't this just the coolest damn thing? +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please do not tag your own article if you are unfamiliar with the tagging process. A member of staff will tag it completely in a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's like a reverse Tannenbombs: instead of rockets that make trees, it's trees that become rockets. Cute and amusing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aside from a few spelling errors and censoring some bits of the containment procedures, this is a pretty interesting article. I feel like it could benefit from some additional information, but as it stands on its own, I think it's unique and amusing to visualize. +1'd!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, this is much better than I was expecting. The idea of trees that propel themselves as a means of seed dispersal is actually pretty fascinating, and I don't see any errors in tone. Although the title needs to be fixed.
